coredata.dc:title: Investigation on the photothermal performance of carbon quantum dots nanofluid with high-stability
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jiaying
Li, Xiaoke
Chen, Wenjing
Cui, Rui
Wu, Xiaohu
coredata.dc:description: The purpose of this paper is to give an in-depth study on the thermos-physical properties and photothermal conversion characteristics of carbon quantum dot (CQDs). In this study, CQDs nanofluids were synthesized by the simple one-step method. A series of stability analysis experiments were conducted to determine the excellent dispersibility and stability of CQDs nanofluids. The thermal conductivity of the nanofluids was measured by KD-2 Pro, and the results showed an enhancement up to 11 % at 50 °C. And the viscosity of CQDs nanofluids decreased with the concentration and temperature. In addition, it was found that the maximum photothermal conversion efficiency was up to 71.09 %. All the results indicated that the prepared CQDs nanofluids had great potential in the applications of direct absorption solar collectors (DASC).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofluid
Solar energy
Carbon quantum dots
DASC

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CQDs nanofluids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of CQDs preparation process (a) The optical path of laser light in deionized water and CQDs nanofluids (R5, R25, R45). The infrared thermographs of (b) R5 (c) R25 and (d) R45.
Schematic diagram of thermal conductivity test system.
Diagram of experimental device for photothermal conversion of CQDs.

(a) TEM images with scale bar in 200 nm of CQDs synthesized by 45 min of microwave heating (The upper left inset is enlarged image with scale bar in 50 nm) (b) Particle size distribution of CQDs by 45 min of microwave heating (c) Schematic image of the dispersed CQDs in PEG-200/GL and their hydrodynamic diameter.
The FT-IR spectra of GL and CQDs synthesized by 30 min of microwave heating.
Statics experimental results (a) taken after preparation (b) 15 days after preparation.
Zeta potential of R45.
Backscattering of R45.
The thermal conductivity of CQDs varies with temperature.
TC enhancement of CQDs varies with temperature.
Viscosity of Glycerol and R45 at 25 °C as a function of shear rate.
(a) transmittance of the sample (b) absorbance of the sample (diluted 100 times).
Contrast diagram of extinction coefficient (diluted 100 times).

(a) R45 under UV light (b) Glycerol under UV light (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of the diluted CQDs (exited at 360 nm) (d) R45 pH-dependent fluorescence emission spectra (exited at 360 nm) (all diluted 100 times).
SER value relative to time of (a) R5 (b) R25 and (c) R45, respectively.
Photothermal conversion efficiency of different fluids.

Temperature distribution curves at different heights and concentrations for the CQDs nanofluids after irradiation time of 3000 s (The solid line does not represent data, but only serves as a trend indicator.).
Calibration of thermal conductivity.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Thermal conductivity measurements calibration with standard glycerol [36].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Viscosity between base liquid and CQDs at different temperatures.
The value of the constant term in the formula.
Total energy absorbed by nanofluid (R5).

head.ce:title: Investigation on the photothermal performance of carbon quantum dots nanofluid with high-stability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: No.1, Dongsan Road, Erxianqiao, Chenghua District, Chengdu 610059, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: No.1, Dongsan Road, Erxianqiao, Chenghua District

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The purpose of this paper is to give an in-depth study on the thermos-physical properties and photothermal conversion characteristics of carbon quantum dot (CQDs). In this study, CQDs nanofluids were synthesized by the simple one-step method. A series of stability analysis experiments were conducted to determine the excellent dispersibility and stability of CQDs nanofluids. The thermal conductivity of the nanofluids was measured by KD-2 Pro, and the results showed an enhancement up to 11 % at 50 °C. And the viscosity of CQDs nanofluids decreased with the concentration and temperature. In addition, it was found that the maximum photothermal conversion efficiency was up to 71.09 %. All the results indicated that the prepared CQDs nanofluids had great potential in the applications of direct absorption solar collectors (DASC).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Till date, energy is still an essential cornerstone of the science development in modern society and the demand for energy in modern technology is inevitably increasing. However, the consumption of traditional energies such as oil, coal and gas are always followed by a series of environment issues including acid rain, dust storms, smog and global warming [1,2]. Lately, the “Emission Peak and Carbon Neutralization” policy was put forward in the world, and the development of clean and renewable energy is an important part of it. Today, renewable energy comes from natural resources such as wind, geothermal and solar energy, can be set as an ideal alternative to the conventional fossil fuels [3,4]. Among them, the solar energy with its rich resources, wide distribution, renewable and pollution-free advantages is highly valued by scholars. At present, the application of solar energy mainly includes photochemistry [5], photovoltaic (PV) technologies, and photo-thermal techniques [6]. The solar-thermal conversion is a simple and straight forward method to absorb and store solar energy. A solar collector is usually used to collect, convert and store thermal radiant energy [2]. However, the thermal resistance and energy loss in the process of converting radiation into internal energy are undesirable, because it will greatly reduce the working efficiency of the collector. Thereby, the direct absorb solar collector (DASC) was proposed by Minardi and Chuang [7] in 1970s, which can absorb the solar energy by using heat transfer fluid (HTFs) [7] directly. However, the most used HTFs such as water, glycol and oil are weak absorbers over the solar spectrum [8].

Nanofluids, defined as a suspensions of nanoparticles (1–100 nm), were firstly coined out by Choi et al. [9] in 1995. Because of the special optical properties, good thermal properties as well as nanoscale effect of nanoparticles, the optical absorption property, heat capacity and energy conversion efficiency of nanofluids are vastly higher than base liquids [10–13]. Till now, there are a large number of experiments showed that the nanofluids can improve the efficiency of DASCs [14]. For instance, Sanjay Kumar et al. [15] prepared water-based gold nanofluids (0.0002 wt%) to improve the thermal efficiency. And the results showed that thermal efficiency of the collector is approximately 33 % higher than that of deionized water at optimal flow rates. In addition to noble metal nanoparticles, metal oxide particles are also commonly used to prepare nanofluids. Wang et al. [16] investigated the heat transfer performance of EG/water based TiO2 nanofluids. At 60 °C, an increment of 36 % was found on the thermal conductivity (TC) of the 0.1 vol% nanofluids compared with the base fluid. Gupta et al. [17] used the water-based Al2O3 nanofluids (~0.005 vol%) to improve the efficiency of DASCs. And the results exhibited an efficiency enhancement of 8.1 % with 1.5 L·m−1 flow rate of nanofluid. Moreover, the performance of carbon nanofluids in this application is not inferior to that of metal nanofluids. Mohammad et al. [18] utilized graphene oxide (GO) nanofluids (0.2 wt%) and alumina (Al2O3) nanofluids to enhance the thermal efficiency of DASC. And they got the results that the improvement efficiency was 63.2 % with GO nanofluids and 32.1 % in Al2O3 nanofluid, respectively. Simultaneously, a challenge associated with the use of metallic oxide such as Al2O3 and TiO2 nanofluids in industrial applications is the potential mechanical erosion and chemical corrosion [19]. Therefore, in order to further improve the application possibility of nanofluids technology in DASCs, it is urgent to develop new nano-materials and study the corresponding photothermal mechanism.

As one of the most necessary and common materials on Earth, carbonaceous materials have always played an important role in the development of modern science and technology [20]. Carbonaceous materials like carbon nanotubes and graphene are considered as potential materials in the use of photothermal conversion [21], but some drawbacks are found to use them. For instance, the multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) nanofluid was prepared by using a procedure for more than one day, including ultrasonic blending, surfactant addition, static settling and drying [22]. Hazra et al. [23] prepared carbon black-ethylene glycol (CB-EG) nanofluids by a time-consuming two-step process for over 12 h. Moreover, surfactant is inevitably used in the preparation process, which will virtually increase the cost of nanofluids technology. The water-based graphene oxide (GO) nanofluid were synthesized by using several components, including l-ascorbic acid, KMnO4, H2SO4, and H2O2. On this basis, the target product can be obtained only after several centrifugation and washing, filtration and ultrasonic dispersion [24]. Therefore, to find a high-stable, non-toxic, and cheap carbonaceous nanofluids with simple preparation process methods should arise extensive attention.

Carbon Quantum Dots (<10 nm) are novel zero-dimensional (0-D) carbon nanomaterial, which is usually consist of crystalline amorphous materials [25]. As a shining star in the nanocarbon family, CQDs process the following excellent features: good water-solubility, low toxicity and easy synthesis [26]. Moreover, CQDs exhibit high and broad absorption efficiency in the visible range, enhancing the solar energy obtained from the corresponding nanofluids compared to conventional base liquid [27]. Simultaneously, CQDs also possess good antibacterial and anti-corrosion properties [28], so it can prolong the service life of the collectors in practical application. Ettefaghi et al. [29] used microwave method to prepare CQDs nanoparticles, and then dispersed them into the car radiator coolant (CRC) to obtain nanofluids. The results indicated the improvement of TC is 5.7 %. Saa et al. [30] prepared EG/water-based (70:30) functionalized carbon dot (F-CQD) and conducted a series of studies on it. The results revealed that the maximum enhancement of TC was 21 % and the improvement of convective heat transfer coefficient was 34 %. Sedaghat et al. [31] measured the thermophysical properties of EG/Water-based (40:60) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanofluids. They found that the TC of nanofluids was enhanced by 18 % with the presence of GQDs nanofluids. However, only a few studies focused on the photothermal properties of CQDs. Joseph and Thomas [32] directly synthesized CQDs nanofluids by one-step method. Additionally, the authors found that the CQDs nanofluids could be stable for more than six months and the estimated cost per thermal efficiency of the CQDs nanofluids based collector was $ 0.0123. The influence of nanofluids on DASC was analyzed through a parabolic collector system and the results showed that the maximum thermal efficiency of the collector was 73.41 %. Seifikar and Azizian [33] made a study on polyethylene glycol-200 (PEG-200) based CQDs nanofluids. The results revealed that the fluid temperature rose rapidly to 50 °C after 10 min of sunlight exposure. Nevertheless, the colloid stability, thermal properties and photothermal properties of CQDs play important roles in the application of DASC, and they deserve further and more comprehensive study.

Generally, polyethylene glycols (PEGs) and glycerol (GL) can be regarded as suitable precursors for CQDs preparation. PEGs are a long chain polymer containing carbon atoms, which can be used as a carbon source. They are non-toxic and highly biodegradable, and are environmentally friendly polymer. Meanwhile, the GL with cheaper price is also environmentally friendly despite its short chain structure. Moreover, Liu et al. [34] found that with the addition of GL, the freezing point of nanofluids drops to about −50 °C which makes it possible to use CQDs nanofluid in cryogenic environments. Considering the above factors, this paper will combine PEG and GL as the precursor to prepare CQDs nanofluids.

In this paper, stable CQDs nanofluids were prepared by one-step method using microwave heating. The nanofluids were characterized and the optical properties was measured. In addition, the stability of the nanofluids was detected. Furthermore, the photothermal conversion experiment of CQDs was conducted and the photothermal conversion efficiency was calculated and discussed. Meanwhile, TC and rheology of CQDs nanofluids are also studied here.
Glycerol, polyethylene glycol-200, hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Ke-long chemical Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CQDs nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , the CQDs nanofluids were prepared by one-step method. In a typical synthesis, 25 mL of PEG-200 was mixed with 75 mL of GL under stirring to form the precursor solution (R0) in a beaker. Then the solution was put into a domestic microwave oven at 700 W and heated for different time periods. Samples heated for 5, 25 and 45 min were labeled as R5, R25 and R45, respectively. The optical path of laser light in the samples are compared in Fig. 1(a). And it indicates the Tyndall effect for the CQDs nanofluids which means the obtained samples are dispersion of CQDs in PEG and GL and formation of the stable nanofluids. The infrared thermal images (Fig. 1(b)–(d)) of the samples taken by the infrared camera showed the temperature just after turning-off of the microwave irradiation. The temperature of the sample is up to 269 °C.

The dipolar polarization mechanism is the primary factors that leads to liquid temperature rise. Because there are many dipoles in PEG [33], the interaction between dipoles and microwave electric field will produce heat [35]. Therefore, during microwave radiation, the temperature rises sharply, resulting in the carbonization of PEG and GL to form CQDs.

In addition, the morphology of CQDs was analyzed by using transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Talos F200S Super-X). And the size distribution of CQDs in nanofluids was confirmed by a dynamic light scattering device (DLS) (Master sizer 2000, Malvern Instruments, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CQDs were obtained by a FT-IR spectrophotometer (WQF-520, Beijing Ruili, China). And the spectra were collected a range from 4000 to 500 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

Firstly, the static precipitation experiment was carried out to qualitatively evaluate the stability of nanofluids. In addition, a Zeta potential measurer, whose uncertainties were under 5 %, was employed to quantificationally predict the suspension stability of CQDs nanofluids. The Turbiscan LabExpert (TLAB, Beijing science and Technology Co., Ltd.) was further used to investigate the stability of R45, which is an optical instrument with a transmission detector and a backward scattering detector. The optical probe scans every 30 s with the height of the sample (up to 55 mm), and backscattering light (ΔBS) data were collected every 40 μm height. The stability of the nanofluids can be confirmed by analyzing ΔBS of the nanofluid.

A KD2-Pro (Decagon Devices, Inc., USA) was used to measure the TC of CQDs nanofluids. Before the experiment, the sensor had to be calibrated with standard glycerol at a certain temperature. The accuracy of the sensor was higher than 98 % (Table 1 ). It should be pointed out that the optimum operating temperature of the sensor was below 50 °C.

The typical method of measuring the TC of nanofluids is shown in Fig. 2 . Firstly, the KS-1 sensor should be completely immersed in the samples vertically because it was both temperature sensor and linear hear source. Then, to achieve equilibrium, the container and sensor should be kept in a thermostatic water bath for 15 min. The above process should be repeated three times, and the average value was taken as the final experimental data.

A HAAKE MARS III rheometer. Was employed to confirm the rheology of nanofluids. It was used along with HAAKE Phoenix 2 temperature controller to keep the temperature at 25 °C. About 10 mL of the nanofluid sample was poured onto the test plate then the shear rate changed from 10 to 2000 s−1 continuously. The experimental data was recorded by RheoWin 3 software.

Because the high concentration of carbon quantum dot nanofluid is too dark in color, the light source are mostly absorbed by the sample [37], resulting in possible bias on experiment of fluorescence and UV–Vis spectra (UV–Vis-NIR, Alpha-1860, China). Therefore, in order to avoid the self-absorption effect of the solution, diluted nanofluids are used in the test of optical properties. The prepared samples (diluted 100 times) were measured by UV–Vis spectra in the wavelength range of 190–1100 nm. The optical properties were studied and the accuracy was ±1 nm. The 10 mm quartz cuvette was used in this part (25 °C).

Fluorescence spectroscopy was collected by a F-7000 spectrometer (HITACHI High-Technologies Corporation, Japan). The fluorescence of the diluted sample was detected. By adding different volumes of HCl or NaOH to adjust the pH of samples (pH = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10) in to the sample, Then, pH-dependent fluorescence spectra (exited at 365 nm) emission was drawn out.

The experimental devices shown in Fig. 3 were used to measure the photo-thermal conversion of nanofluids. First, insulation material was used to wrap around the bottom and surrounding of the quartz beaker to reduce the heat loss. The samples were placed in a beaker with a transparent quartz glass sheet cover on it to prevent the evaporation of the fluid. During the experiment, a Xenon lamp was used to simulate sunlight, meeting AAA standard and AM 1.5 spectrum. Then, the intensity of the simulated light was measured with a solar radiometer. To assess the temperature distribution of the nanofluid, four K-type thermocouples (sensitivity: ±0.1 °C) were installed at different heights of the quartz beaker (1, 2, 3, 4 cm from the bottom, respectively) and it was linked to a data acquisition device (JK80X) through a computer to record temperature changes at intervals of 10 s. The totally recording time was 3000 s.

The influence of CQDs nanofluids on the photothermal conversion performance was studied through the stored energy ratio (SER). The calculation formula of SER is as follows:
(1)
SER =
T
nf
t
−
T
nf
0
T
bf
t
−
T
bf
0
where T
nf(t) represents temperature of the nanofluids when the time is t, T
nf(t) means the initial temperature of the nanofluids, T
bf(t) represents the temperature of the base fluid when the time is t, and T
bf(t) is the initial temperature of the base fluid.

The photothermal conversion efficiency ŋ of CQDs nanofluids can be calculated by:
(2)
ŋ =
m nf
C nf
∆ T
GA ∆ t
× 100
where ∆T is the difference between the initial temperature and the equilibrium temperature, G means the incoming solar heat flex (W/m2), A is the top surface area of beaker (m2), ∆t represents the time of exposure to the light radiation, m
nf means the weight of the nanofluid and C
nf is the specific heat capacity of the nanofluid. And in this experiment, the light heat flux G value is 2500 (W/m2), and the top surface area is 4.1548 cm2.

According to Eq. (2), the errors of the above parameters are as follows: m
nf is ±0.01 g, C
nf is ±1 %, ∆T is ±0.2 °C, G is ±5 %, A is ±0.01 mm and ∆t is ±1 s. Therefore, the uncertainty formula of photothermal conversion efficiency can be expressed as:
(3)
U η
η
2
=
U m
m
2
+
U c
c
2
+
U
∆ T
∆ T
2
+
U G
G
2
+
U A
A
2
+
U
∆ t
∆ t
2
Through the calibration analysis and based on Eq. (3), the maximum relative error of the photothermal conversion efficiency of the CQDs nanofluids was 1.385 %.

The TEM picture is displayed in Fig. 4(a) which indicates the topographic characteristics of CQDs are near spherical. The normal size distribution curve of CQDs was drawn in Fig. 4(b). It shows the size of CQDs mainly distribute from 10 to 16 nm and the average particle size is 13.0 nm. Moreover, based on DLS analysis, the distribution of hydrodynamic size of CQDs was obtained in Fig. 4(c). The average hydrodynamic diameter (dH) of CQDs is 115 nm while the average diameter (dH) of the CQDs obtained by TEM is 13 nm. This is due to the protective shell (about 50 nm) surrounding around the CQDs particles. The aggregation of nanoparticles is one of the important reasons for the enhancement of thermal conductivity of nanofluids [38]. Particle aggregation essentially results in areas of low thermal resistance, ultimately enhancing local heat transfer. However, a larger dH of nanoparticles provides better stability for nanofluids, which means that nanoparticles are more difficult to aggregate, thus hindering TC promotion.

Fig. 5 shows the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum, which reflects the surface functional groups of glycerol and CQDs nanofluids. The peak at 3375 cm−1 for R45 and 3300 for glycerol could be attributed to the stretching vibration of alcoholic hydroxyl group (-OH), which might occur causing by the generation of -OH on CQDs surface. The peak at 2830 cm−1 could be assigned to stretching vibration of -CH. For R45, the peak at 1680 cm−1 could be attributed to stretching modes of CO for carboxyl, because the carbon source used for the synthesis were carbohydrates. It has been mentioned in other articles that CO can be detected between 1600 and 1700 cm−1 [39,40]. And there is CC stretching vibration at 1345 cm−1. The results show that the carbon dots with different surface chemical bonds (C=C) and surface functional groups (e.g. -COOH, -OH) have been successfully prepared which process ideal water-solubility.

The static test results are shown in the Fig. 6 . The results show there is no agglomerates and precipitation generated in the nanofluids after 15 days of preparation. GL contributes to the stability of nanofluids in the following two ways. First, with abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of GL, it shows excellent water solubility. Besides, because of the high viscosity of GL, it reduces the Brownian velocity and thus reducing the chance of particle collisions and agglomeration. For PEG-200, as a modifier and passivator, by introducing more hydroxyl groups it can effectively fill the surface defects of CQDs and passivate their surface [33]. Because the centroid spacing is large (dH = 115 nm), the attraction between particles is small. As a result, the agglomeration is difficult to form and it leads to the CQDs nanoparticles distribute in the solution with good dispersion.

In addition, according to the stability theory of suspensions, the Zeta potential is a measure of the strength of the mutual repulsion or attraction between particles [41]. The higher the absolute value of Zeta potential is, the greater the electrostatic repulsion between particles is, and the less likely the suspension is to delaminate or precipitate. In terms of quantitative analysis, it is generally considered that when the absolute value of zeta potential of suspension is higher than 30 mV, its stability is good [42]. As shown in Fig. 7 , the Zeta potential value is found to be about −43 mV, which indicates that the nanofluids have a high stability. This is also an indication of the presence of deprotonated carboxylic acid groups at the surface of these nanoparticles [43]. Moreover, the Zeta potential changed from −29.0 to −53 mV as the pH value from 2.0 to 10.0. By adding alkaline to the nanofluid, the increase of -OH in the solution and the reaction between the hydroxyl group of the nanoparticles and -OH, resulting in the decrease of the potential of the nanofluids [44].

In addition, the backscattering of R45 is shown in Fig. 8 . The blue line represents the first scan result, and the red line represents the last scan result. At the bottom of the bottle (45–55 mm), due to the agglomeration effect of part quantum dots, it fluctuates obviously. However, the backscattering light (ΔBS) distribution curve of CQDs nanofluids (0–40 nm) do not change significantly with the increase of measurement time, further indicating the ideal stability of CQDs nanofluids.
The variation trend of TC of CQDs nanofluids with temperature is shown in Fig. 9 .

All the nanofluids displayed a decline trend with the temperature. Specifically, the TC of R45 was lower than that of other nanofluids at each temperature. Although, at the low temperature TC of base fluid is higher than that of nanofluids, but with the temperature increasing, the change of TC of nanofluid is less than that of base fluid.

In general, the reasons behind this are (1) The TC of the base liquid (R0) decreases with the increase of temperature, which is determined by the thermos-physical properties itself. (2) The main reason is the Brownian motion, and the collision frequency of nanoparticles becomes more intense with the increase of temperature. And with the increase of concentration of the CQDs particles, the TC deteriorates due to the agglomeration of the nanoparticles at higher concentration [45]. Thus, R45 shows the lowest value of TC. Additionally, the heat in these materials is carried by “the quanta of lattice vibrations” [46] of both electrons and phonons. The phonons have the predominate contribution in thermal conductivity of these materials. But nanoparticles tend to aggregate or settle in the base liquid due to their high surface energy, which is attributed to π-π interactions and van der Waals forces [47]. The phonon transportation can be hindered by the settling and agglomeration of nanoparticles, hence adversely influence the heat transport characteristics of nanofluids. Therefore, the TC of CQDs nanofluids is less than R0 at low temperature due to increased phonon boundary scattering [48].

The TC enhancement of CQDs nanofluids was calculated by Eq. (4):
(4)
TC
enhancement =
k nf
− k
k
× 100 %
where k is the thermal conductivity of glycerol, and k
nf is the thermal conductivity of CQDs nanofluids.

The results calculated by the Eq. (4) are shown in Fig. 10 . A maximum TC enhancement of 11 % was found on R25 nanofluids at 50 °C. Compared with the base fluid, CQDs nanofluids have an obvious improvement in thermal conductivity. Because of the increase of the addition amount of the nanoparticles, it enhances the collision frequency between the nanoparticles. Meanwhile, it is also more favorable for the heat conduction between the solid-solid two phases of the nanofluids and the heat transfer between the nanoparticles and the base fluid. Hence, R25 nanofluids can be used as a good heat conduction medium at high temperature as HTF.

The viscosity values of CQDs nanofluids are exhibited in Table 2 . It can be found that the viscosity of nanofluids decreased with the concentration and temperature. Specifically, at room temperature, the viscosity of R45 is only 41.6 % of the base liquid. And with the increase of temperature, the viscosity of R45 decreases by 73.3 % at most. Clearly the reason of this phenomenon is different from the classic theories such as the Einstein-Batchelor relationship. This law stated that the effective viscosity should increase with the particle loadings [49]. But the viscosity of CQDs nanofluids showed the opposite trend. Here are two possible potential mechanisms of this phenomenon including the free volume effect and particle agglomeration [50–52]. The addition of CQDs destroys the long-chain molecular structure and hydrogen bonds of GL and PEG-200, therefore the viscosity of nanofluids decline. On the other hand, the viscosity of different CQDs nanofluids in various microwaving time will decline as the temperature increase. It is because the inter-particle attractions in liquids are stronger than gas, they have greater freedom to move. By raising temperature, thermal motions of molecules increase, the intramolecular forces weaken and the resistance of shear stress lessen. So once the temperature increases the inter-particle and inter-molecular attractive forces reduced which would allow fluid layers to move more actively. Thus, CQDs synthesis can reduce the viscosity, and this viscosity reduction can ease and speed up the particle motion, which augment TC and thermal exchange property subsequently [53].

The test result for rheology is shown in Fig. 11 and it can be concluded that both R0 and R45 are non-Newtonian fluids. For R0, glycerol tends to form hydrogen bonds between molecules, and too many hydrogen bonds can make it sticky. The long molecular chain is easily entangled, so the increase in shear rate unwinds the entangled molecular chain and simultaneously the dispersion phase particles in the liquid from the disordered arrangement state to the stratified arrangement [54]. Meanwhile, as for R45, when the shear rate is low, there could be error in the results, which can be ignored. Later, with the increase of shear rate and breaking of the agglomerations of the CQDs, R45 becomes shear thinning after the agglomeration disappears which is in agreement with the previous experiment studies [55,56].

In addition, the curve of R0 and R45 was fitted to get Eq. (5). Since the rheometer may have errors at low rotational speed, part of the data of green point in the figure is discarded and the subsequent data are fitted as Eq. (5). The constants in the formula are shown in Table 3 . After fitting, R2 values of R0 and R45 are 0.98 and 0.99, respectively.
(5)
y =
y 0
+
A 1
∗
1 − exp
−
x
t 1
+
A 2
∗
1 − exp
−
x
t 2

In Fig. 12(a), the light transmittance of CQDs nanofluids in the range of 300–850 nm is increased with the concentration. It should be pointed out that if the nanofluids are not diluted 100 times, their light transmittance will exceed the detection limit of the instrument and be close to 0. And the absorbance spectrum gives the comprehensive information about the optical properties of nanofluids (Fig. 12(b)). The absorbance of nanofluids increases with the increase of microwave irradiation time. According to the Beer–Lambert's law [57], the absorbance is proportional to solution nanoparticle concentration. Moreover, absorption peaks are observed at the wavelength of 275nm corresponding to a π → π* transition of CC with sp2 hybridization [58].

In addition, the extinction coefficient α(λ) in the spectral pass rate T(λ) can be calculated according to the Lambert-Beer law. The formula is as follows:
(6)
T
λ
=
e
− α
λ
l
where l means the optical path length of the nanofluid, which was 1 cm in the experiment.

The extinction coefficients of R0, R5, R25 and R45 (190–1100 nm) are shown in Fig. 13 . According to the figure, the synthesized CQDs nanofluids (R45) can improve the extinction coefficient in the wavelength range of 190–600 nm significantly. Compared with R0, the extinction coefficient of nanofluids increased with the microwave radiation time. It is proved effectively that CQDs in nanofluids can improve the extinction coefficient of solution, and the more CQDs, the better the degree of light absorption.

The experimental results of fluorescence test (365 nm) show that R45 is green fluorescence, while R0 shows blue fluorescence. The fluorescence emission spectra of nanofluids were characterized in Fig. 14(c). It was found that the fluorescence emission wavelength did not change at different concentrations and the intensity increased with the microwaving time gradually.

It is acknowledged that CQDs nanofluids have pH responsive fluorescence and could be used as pH sensors in some special circumstances [59]. Subsequently, NaOH and HCl are added into R45 to adjust the pH of the solution so that the effect of pH value on fluorescence intensity can be tested. As is shown in Fig. 14(d), the emission spectra and intensity change of the CQDs nanofluids of same concentration under different pH show different fluorescence intensities. They display similar emission peaks around 460 nm without wavelength shift. The origin sample has a pH of 6, which makes it has the maximum fluorescence intensity and the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases as pH changes. This pH-response of CQDs can be explained by deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of the CQDs. And because of this deprotonation, the H-bonding effect is reduced, which causes the decrease in vibrational coupling. Above phenomenon will lead to more discrete energy levels and result in the decrease of photoluminescent emission [59]. Therefore, CQDs nanofluids are not suitable for use in overly acidic or alkaline environments.

Stored energy ratio (SER) is an indicator which can identify the supplementary energy absorbed by the fluid. SER value of CQDs nanofluids was shown in Fig. 15 . The results show that the SER value of the top layer (H4) of R45 is about 1.73 at 2000 s, indicating that the energy storage on top of R45 is 1.73 times than that of R0. At the deep height (H2,H3), SER was higher than 1 from 550 to 3000 s. In the process of light absorption of CQDs nanofluids, photothermal effect will occur. Generally, the photothermal mechanism consists of three processes: photoelectron excitation, relaxation, and thermal diffusion. The previous characterization showed that CQDs of different sizes exist in the nanofluids, so it can absorb light of different wavelengths. The incident light was absorbed by CQDs and thus the electronic was excited from the valence band (V.B.) to the conduction band (C.B.) which will produce electron-hole pairs [33] After CQDs nanofluids absorbing light within a certain range of solar spectrum, the difference between the energy of the incident photon and the gap between the electrons becomes heat. Therefore, it can improve the efficiency of heat conversion in photothermal conversion experiment.

The photothermal conversion efficiency of CQDs nanofluids are drawn in Fig. 16 . After 3000 s of illumination, the simulated light source provides about 2500 J of energy. However, the base liquid R0 can only absorb 1395 J of energy (55.8 %) while the maximum efficiency of R25 nanofluids is 71.09 %. The photothermal conversion efficiency of all CQDs nanofluids is >67 %, 12 % higher than that of the base liquid, which indicates that CQDs nanofluids are a better photothermal conversion working medium.

Meanwhile, the total energy (E
total ) absorbed by the nanofluid in the process of photothermal conversion was calculated by the following formula:
(7)
E total
=
m nf
C nf
T max
−
T min
where m
nf is the quality of the nanofluids, C
nf represents the heat capacity of the nanofluids, T
max is the highest temperature of the fluid, T
min means the minimum temperature of the fluid.

The temperature distributions inside the working fluids of DASC after 3000 s of illumination are shown in Fig. 17 . The temperature profiles between the top and bottom of the collector increased as the CQDs nanofluids microwaving time increasing became higher. This is due to the increased concentration of the nanofluid, which darkens the color of the liquid and thus reduces the transmittance. And it reflects that R45 has excellent surface solar photothermal conversion performance and can be used as DASC heat transfer fluid.

Such a results can be shown in Table 4 below. It shows that the top layer of CQDs nanofluids has a high photothermal conversion efficiency, which can effectively convert light into heat. Moreover, compared with R0, the CQDs nanofluids have a significant increase in energy absorption. Overall, glycerol-based CQDs nanofluids present excellent photothermal performances that can be applied in real applications based on DASC.
In this paper, CQDs nanofluids were prepared by microwave method using GL and PEG-200 as raw materials. After a series of tests, the following conclusions are drawn:
(1) The prepared CQDs nanofluids exhibited good stability because the surface of CQDs was passivated by adding GL and PEG-200.
(2) The test results of thermal conductivity and viscosity of CQDs nanofluids showed that they have the potential to be used as good HTFs.
(3) The CQDs nanofluids have excellent light absorption property in a wide band, especially in the infrared region, so they can effectively improve the conversion rate of solar energy.
(4) The energy storage capacity of CQDs nanofluids is higher than GL. Moreover, the maximum photothermal conversion efficiency of CQDs was found to be 69.89 %.
Jiaying Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-Original draft preparation.
Xiaoke Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Visualization, Review & editing.
Wenjing Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Review & editing.
Rui Cui: Data Curation.
Xiaohu Wu: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support of Supported by Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2022YFG0306)，Chengdu University of Information Technology (KYTZ202117) and Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (2022NSFSC1238). And the authors would like to thank Jingsui Liu from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the technical support.
